content.problemStatement:
Chef has an array AAA of size NNN. He can perform the following operation on AAA:


Select an iii (1≤i≤N)(1 \le i \le N)(1≤i≤N) and for all 1≤j≤i1 \le j \le i1≤j≤i, set Aj:=Aj+1A_j := A_j + 1Aj​:=Aj​+1 (i.e. add 111 to every element in the prefix of length iii).
Chef wants to convert AAA to a palindrome by using the above operation **minimum** number of times. Can you help Chef?

If it is not possible to convert AAA to a palindrome, output −1-1−1.

Note: An array is called palindrome if it reads the same backwards and forwards, for e.g. [1,4,1][1, 4, 1][1,4,1] and [7,3,3,7][7, 3, 3, 7][7,3,3,7] are palindromic arrays.


Input Format

The first line contains a single integer TTT — the number of test cases. Then the test cases follow.
The first line of each test case contains an integer NNN — the size of the array AAA.
The second line of each test case contains NNN space-separated integers A1,A2,…,ANA_1, A_2, \dots, A_NA1​,A2​,…,AN​ denoting the array AAA.


Output Format
For each test case, output the **minimum** number of operations required to convert AAA to a palindromic array.


If it is not possible to do so, output −1-1−1.
constraints: Sum of NNN over all test cases does not exceed 3⋅1053 \cdot 10^53⋅105.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Test case 111: The given array is already a palindrome.
Test case 222: It can be proven that it is not possible to convert AAA to a palindromic array using the given operation.
Test case 333: We can perform the following operations:

Select i=1i = 1i=1, [1,2,3,4]→[2,2,3,4][1, 2, 3, 4] \rightarrow [2, 2, 3, 4][1,2,3,4]→[2,2,3,4]
Select i=2i = 2i=2, [2,2,3,4]→[3,3,3,4][2, 2, 3, 4] \rightarrow [3, 3, 3, 4][2,2,3,4]→[3,3,3,4]
Select i=1i = 1i=1, [3,3,3,4]→[4,3,3,4][3, 3, 3, 4] \rightarrow [4, 3, 3, 4][3,3,3,4]→[4,3,3,4]
